title: Power BI - Semantic Modeling, Relationships, And DAX Conventions
applies_to: Power BI relationships, measures, and DAX-based analytics

confirmed_facts.fact: Relationships between tables are necessary to accurately calculate results and display the correct information in Power BI reports, and Power BI Desktop can autodetect relationships during load.
Relationship options include cardinality, cross-filter direction, and whether a relationship is active.
Power BI Desktop supports many-to-many relationship scenarios and commonly models them with a bridging table.
Measures are DAX-based calculations whose results change as users interact with reports, making them central to dynamic analysis in Power BI.
Power BI Desktop supports model measures, report-level measures, quick measures, display folders, and dynamic format strings for measures.
DAX is the formula language used to create calculations, logic, and analysis in Power BI semantic models.

implementation_notes: Treat semantic modeling as the foundation for reliable report design. Visual quality cannot compensate for a weak relationship model.
Default to star-schema thinking where practical: facts for measurable events, dimensions for slicing and filtering.
Use measures rather than calculated columns for dynamic aggregations and KPI logic whenever the calculation should respond to filter context.
Document relationship direction, cardinality, and active or inactive status explicitly when handing off a Power BI design to another engineer or analyst.
Use bridging tables deliberately for many-to-many scenarios instead of hoping visuals infer the intended business logic.
caveats: Autodetect helps, but it is not a substitute for deliberate model review.
Many-to-many relationships solve real problems but can also make model behavior harder to reason about if used casually.
DAX and report-level measures add flexibility, but they also increase maintenance burden if naming and organization are weak.
